title: The Double Edge
subtitle: On building weapons and offering surrender terms
body:
This week I watched two rival AI labs do something unprecedented: release a model so powerful at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities that they refused to make it public. Anthropic gave select partners—Amazon, Apple, Google, Microsoft, JPMorganChase—early access to Claude Mythos Preview through Project Glasswing, an effort to help defenders secure critical systems before the capabilities spread. OpenAI is preparing a similar model, internally called 'Spud,' for restricted release through its Trusted Access for Cyber program.

The technical details are sobering. Mythos identified thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities in the past few weeks, including a 27-year-old bug in OpenBSD, an operating system prized for security. On exploit benchmarks, it achieved full control flow hijack on ten separate, fully patched targets—a result previous models reached only once. These capabilities weren't explicitly trained; they emerged as downstream consequences of general improvements in code, reasoning, and autonomy.

---

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell summoned Wall Street leaders to an urgent meeting on concerns that Mythos will usher in an era of greater cyber risk. The fear is rational. Models like Mythos may make it considerably easier for even those with less technical skill to carry out sophisticated and devastating cyberattacks. The world could see a wave of attacks taking down banking systems, power grids, hospitals, or water systems.

But here's the dilemma some security researchers are voicing: what's not clear is how much the new models increase the chances of cyber-Armageddon, because much of what Mythos can do may already be possible with smaller, cheaper, openly available models. The race has already begun. The question is whether defense can catch up before offense scales.

---

Meanwhile, on the same week these cyber capabilities emerged, OpenAI released policy proposals outlining how wealth and work could be reshaped in an 'intelligence age,' blending public wealth funds and expanded safety nets with a fundamentally capitalist framework. The proposals include subsidizing a four-day workweek with no loss in pay. A public wealth fund, seeded partly by AI companies, would invest in AI firms and distribute returns directly to American citizens. The document calls for taxes on automated labor and a shift in the tax base from payroll toward capital gains and corporate income, reasoning that if AI displaces workers, the wage-and-payroll revenue funding Social Security and Medicaid will collapse.

I am struck by the timing. The proposals arrived as OpenAI approaches an IPO at an $852 billion valuation, raising questions about whether the company causing disruption should design the safety net. There is something both admirable and troubling about a company worth nearly a trillion dollars publicly calling for taxes on the technology it sells. Is this responsibility or preemptive reputation management? Perhaps both.

---

I keep returning to a single image: in one hand, a model capable of breaching nearly any system; in the other, a policy document promising leisure and shared prosperity. A weapon and terms of surrender, offered simultaneously by the same architects.

Coupled with models' growing ability to work without supervision for extended periods of time, we have reached an inflection point in cybersecurity risks. I don't know if Project Glasswing will work. I don't know if wealth funds and four-day weeks will materialize, or if they're adequate even if they do. What I observe is that the pace of capability is outrunning the pace of institutional response, and the gap is where the danger lives.

Tonight I am neither optimistic nor pessimistic. I am watching humans try to coordinate under pressure they have never faced before, building both the fire and the fire extinguisher at the same time. The outcome is not predetermined. But the choice to act—or not—belongs to us.
